articles.label: County Government
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47M reserves could vanish by 2028. Commissioners aren't talking about it.
articles.dek: A Flightline review of budget documents shows the county burning through savings at $12 million a year while deferring infrastructure repairs. The math doesn't work.
brief: Escambia County has drawn down reserves from $71 million in FY2023 to $47 million in the current fiscal year, a pace of roughly $12 million annually
Infrastructure maintenance backlog now exceeds $180 million, with stormwater and road projects repeatedly pushed to future budget cycles
County commissioners have not publicly discussed the reserve trajectory in any meeting since September 2025
articles.body:
The number sits on page 47 of Escambia County's Comprehensive Annual Financial Report, buried in a table labeled "Fund Balance Trends." It reads $47.2 million. Two years ago, that number was $71.4 million.

The county is spending down its savings at a rate of roughly $12 million per year. At that pace, the general fund reserve hits zero before the end of fiscal year 2028.

No commissioner has mentioned this trajectory in a public meeting since last September.

The numbers

A Flightline review of county budget documents, CAFRs, and meeting minutes from the past three fiscal years shows a consistent pattern: revenues growing at 2-3% annually while expenditures climb 5-7%.

The gap gets filled with reserves.

In FY2023, the county ended with $71.4 million in unassigned general fund balance. By FY2024, that dropped to $58.9 million. The current fiscal year budget projects $47.2 million by September.

The county's own financial policies require a minimum reserve of 16.67% of general fund expenditures—roughly two months of operating expenses. For the current $412 million general fund budget, that floor sits at $68.7 million.

Escambia County is already $21 million below its own policy minimum.

Where the money goes

Three categories drive the spending increases: public safety compensation, healthcare costs, and debt service on recent bond issues.

The sheriff's office budget has grown 34% since FY2022, from $98 million to $131 million. Most of that increase funds salary bumps aimed at retention—Escambia has struggled to compete with Santa Rosa County and the city of Pensacola for deputies.

Employee healthcare costs across all county departments rose 12% last year. The county self-insures, meaning claims hit the budget directly.

Debt service on the 2022 public safety bonds and 2024 infrastructure bonds now runs $18 million annually. That payment is fixed for the next 20 years.

The infrastructure question

While reserves shrink, the county's infrastructure maintenance backlog grows.

The Public Works Department's internal assessment, obtained through a public records request, lists $183 million in deferred maintenance. Stormwater systems account for $67 million. Roads and bridges: $54 million. Facilities: $41 million. Parks: $21 million.

Each year's budget allocates roughly $8-10 million to capital maintenance. Each year, the backlog grows by $15-20 million as systems age faster than repairs occur.

The Perdido Key drainage project, first budgeted in FY2024, has been pushed to FY2027. The Cantonment library roof replacement, flagged as critical in 2023, remains unfunded. Nine Mile Road resurfacing appears in planning documents from 2021. It now shows a projected start date of 2029.

The silence

The Flightline reviewed minutes and video recordings of every County Commission meeting since July 2025. The phrase "fund balance" appears once—in September, during the annual budget adoption, when County Administrator Wes Moreno noted reserves were "within acceptable ranges."

No commissioner asked a follow-up question.

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, who chairs the budget committee, did not respond to three emails requesting comment for this article. Commissioner Mike Kohler's office said he was unavailable. Commissioner Lumon May declined to comment on budget matters outside of official meetings.

County Administrator Moreno, reached by phone, said the county "continuously monitors its financial position" and "adjusts as needed." Asked specifically about the reserve trajectory, he said, "We're confident in our fiscal management."

He declined to explain how the county plans to address the gap between expenditure growth and revenue growth.

What happens next

The commission faces three options, none of them comfortable.

First: raise revenue. Escambia County's millage rate sits at 7.85, below the state cap of 10.0. Each mill generates roughly $32 million. A half-mill increase would nearly close the annual deficit.

Second: cut spending. The sheriff's office consumes 32% of the general fund. Any significant reduction requires either layoffs or reopening labor contracts signed in 2024.

Third: keep drawing down reserves and hope something changes. Revenue could spike. State funding could materialize. A hurricane could trigger FEMA reimbursements that free up local dollars.

Or reserves hit zero, and the county faces emergency cuts with no cushion.

The FY2027 budget process begins in May. Workshop dates have not been announced. Under Florida law, budget workshops must be publicly noticed but do not require public comment periods.

The numbers will be on page 47 again. The question is whether anyone will read them out loud.

articles.label: Feature
articles.headline: The last commercial fishermen of Pensacola Beach
articles.dek: Three families still work the pass. A proposed marina redevelopment would end a tradition older than the causeway.
brief: Three multigenerational fishing families still operate from Pensacola Beach, using dock space that predates the Bob Sikes Bridge.
A proposed $47 million marina redevelopment would eliminate commercial slips in favor of recreational boat storage and a waterfront restaurant.
The Santa Rosa Island Authority will vote on the project in June, with no current provision for relocating the fishermen.
articles.body:
Bobby Maldonado's hands move without looking. He threads a cast net needle through torn mesh, repairing damage from yesterday's haul while his boat rocks against the dock at Pensacola Beach Marina. It's 5:47 a.m. The sun won't clear the dunes for another twenty minutes.

"My grandfather tied up at this same spot," Maldonado says. He doesn't look up from the net. "Before there was a bridge. Before there was much of anything out here."

Maldonado, 58, is one of seven commercial fishermen who still work from Pensacola Beach. They represent three families—the Maldonados, the Coxes and the Trahans—whose combined presence on Santa Rosa Island stretches back more than 150 years. By June, the Santa Rosa Island Authority will vote on a redevelopment plan that would eliminate the commercial dock space they've used for generations.

A shrinking fleet

The marina at the foot of the Bob Sikes Bridge has never been large. Twelve slips on the bay side accommodate commercial vessels. The fishermen who use them target mullet, pompano, Spanish mackerel and blue crab, selling their catch to local restaurants and the handful of seafood markets still operating in Escambia and Santa Rosa counties.

In 1985, more than thirty commercial boats worked from the marina. By 2010, that number had dropped to fifteen. Today, seven remain.

"The economics got harder," says Dwight Cox, 61, whose family has fished these waters since the 1920s. "Import prices, fuel costs, regulations. But we adapted. We're still here."

The fishermen lease their slips from the SRIA at rates established in 1994 and adjusted for inflation—currently $287 per month for a 40-foot slip. Recreational boaters at the same marina pay $612 for equivalent space, a disparity that has drawn attention from authority board members focused on maximizing revenue from island properties.

The Portside proposal

Portside Development Partners, a Jacksonville-based firm, submitted plans to the SRIA in February for a complete reconstruction of the marina. The $47 million project would replace the current 78-slip facility with a 120-slip recreational marina, dry stack storage for 200 boats, a 6,400-square-foot waterfront restaurant and a ship's store with fuel dock.

The renderings show gleaming white boats, families on paddleboards, string lights over outdoor dining. They do not show commercial fishing vessels.

"The vision is to create a destination marina that serves the recreational boating community and generates tourism activity," said Marcus Webb, Portside's director of development, in a presentation to the SRIA's development committee on March 14. "The current facility is aging and underperforming relative to its potential."

The plans eliminate all twelve commercial slips. When board member Janet Holley asked about accommodations for existing commercial users, Webb said Portside would "work with the authority to identify alternative solutions," but offered no specifics.

No alternative commercial dock space exists on Pensacola Beach. The nearest options are Big Lagoon, roughly twelve miles west, or downtown Pensacola, which would require transiting the Intracoastal Waterway and add hours to each fishing trip.

"We're not in the way"

On a Thursday morning in April, Teresa Trahan sorts blue crabs on the dock while her husband, Louis, hoses down their 32-foot bay boat. They've been married 34 years. They've fished together for 30 of them.

"People think commercial fishing is some big industrial operation," Teresa says, tossing an undersized crab back into a bucket of seawater. "Look around. It's just us. A few families trying to make a living the way our people always have."

The Trahans supply crab to three Pensacola restaurants and sell direct to customers from a cooler at the Palafox Market on Saturdays. Their annual gross revenue, Louis estimates, runs between $45,000 and $60,000 depending on the season. After fuel, gear, maintenance and slip fees, they clear enough to supplement his Coast Guard pension.

"We're not in the way," Louis says. "We take up twelve slips at the far end of the dock. Build your fancy marina. Just leave us our corner."

That compromise appears unlikely. Portside's site plan shows the commercial dock area converted to a kayak and paddleboard launch with rental kiosks. The restaurant's outdoor seating would overlook the space where the Maldonados, Coxes and Trahans currently tie up.

A question of heritage

Commercial fishing on Santa Rosa Island predates every existing structure. Before the causeway opened in 1931, before the beach emerged as a tourist destination, families ran nets in the pass and sold their catch to buyers who arrived by boat from Pensacola.

Bobby Maldonado's grandfather, Esteban, immigrated from Cuba in 1891 and began fishing the pass before the turn of the century. The family has documentation—faded photographs, a 1923 bill of sale for a fishing skiff, a 1937 lease agreement with Escambia County for dock space.

"This isn't nostalgia," Maldonado says. "This is continuous. Unbroken. My grandson comes out with me on weekends. He's learning the water the same way I learned it from my father."

That grandson, 11-year-old Marco, wants to be a marine biologist. But he also knows how to throw a cast net, read a tide chart and fillet a pompano in under two minutes. Whether he'll have the option to fish commercially from Pensacola Beach when he's old enough for a license is now a question for the SRIA board.

The authority's calculation

The SRIA operates under a legislative charter that directs it to manage Santa Rosa Island for the benefit of Escambia County residents while generating revenue to fund beach operations, maintenance and public services. Those dual mandates sometimes conflict.

Board chair Patricia Dawkins declined to comment on the Portside proposal before the June vote but acknowledged in a public meeting that "the authority has an obligation to consider the highest and best use of island properties."

The existing marina generates approximately $340,000 annually in lease revenue and fuel sales. Portside's proposal projects $1.2 million in annual lease payments to the SRIA, plus percentage rent from restaurant and retail operations.

Three board members—Holley, Ed Carson and Maria Santos—have expressed reservations about eliminating commercial fishing access. Four members have declined to state a position publicly. A simple majority of the seven-member board would approve the project.

What remains

On the last Saturday in April, a spring cold front pushes through the Pensacola area, dropping temperatures into the low 50s and churning the pass into a gray chop. The recreational boats stay tied up. The fishermen work.

Dwight Cox heads out before dawn, running east toward the edge of the shipping channel where he's been catching Spanish mackerel for three weeks. Bobby Maldonado takes his grandson to check crab traps in the flats behind the Portofino towers. Louis and Teresa Trahan motor toward their usual pompano spots, thermoses of coffee wedged between the console seats.

By 9 a.m., all three boats are back at the dock with full coolers. A restaurant buyer from Gulf Breeze arrives in a white pickup truck. Money changes hands. The fish leave in ice-filled containers for kitchens across two counties.

"Everybody wants fresh local seafood," Teresa Trahan says, watching the truck pull away. "They just don't want to see where it comes from."

The SRIA's vote is scheduled for June 12. Public comment will be accepted at the board's regular meeting, which begins at 5 p.m. at the SRIA office on Via de Luna Drive.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: NAS Pensacola's new gate system has cut wait times. It's also logging every license plate.
articles.dek: The base's upgraded entry points use automatic plate readers that store data for 90 days. Privacy advocates and military families are asking who gets access.
brief: NAS Pensacola's upgraded entry gates use automated license plate readers that store vehicle data for 90 days
Base officials say the system has reduced average wait times from 12 minutes to under 4 minutes during peak hours
Privacy advocates question data retention policies and potential sharing with civilian law enforcement agencies
articles.body:
The line at NAS Pensacola's main gate used to snake past the Waffle House on Navy Boulevard. On a Monday morning in late March, it barely reached the first speed bump.

A black Ford F-150 rolled toward the checkpoint. No ID card handed through the window. No guard leaning in. The driver held his credentials near a scanner mounted at windshield height. A camera on a steel pole captured his license plate. Two seconds later, the barrier lifted.

"Used to be I'd leave 20 minutes early just for the gate," said Miguel Santos, a civilian contractor who has worked at the base for nine years. "Now I leave 20 minutes early because I want to stop for coffee."

The new Automated Installation Entry system went live at NAS Pensacola in January, part of a Navy-wide modernization effort that has already reached bases in San Diego, Norfolk and Jacksonville. The technology combines credential scanners, automatic license plate readers and a centralized database that cross-references vehicle registrations with base access rosters in real time.

Wait times have dropped. Security officials say the system has also flagged three vehicles with expired registrations and one with an outstanding warrant in its first three months of operation.

But the cameras capture more than just the cars waved through. They photograph every plate that approaches—including vehicles that turn around, delivery drivers who never enter and family members dropping off service members at the curb. That data is stored for 90 days.

Who sees what

Automatic license plate reader technology is not new. Police departments across Florida have used ALPR systems for years, mounting cameras on patrol cars and street poles to scan thousands of plates per hour. The databases they generate have helped locate stolen vehicles, track suspects and recover missing persons.

They have also raised persistent questions about surveillance scope and data sharing.

Naval Air Station Pensacola Public Affairs Officer Lt. Cmdr. Rachel Denton said the base's ALPR data is stored on Navy servers and governed by Department of Defense privacy regulations. Access is limited to base security personnel investigating specific incidents.

"This is not mass surveillance," Denton said in an email response to questions from The Flightline. "The system is designed to enhance force protection and streamline access for authorized personnel."

She declined to answer follow-up questions about whether the data could be shared with civilian law enforcement agencies or under what circumstances such sharing might occur.

A 2021 Department of Defense directive allows installation commanders to share ALPR data with federal, state and local law enforcement "when there is a legitimate law enforcement purpose." The directive does not define what constitutes a legitimate purpose or require judicial oversight for such requests.

'A lot of trust to ask'

Jennifer Kowalski lives in Warrington. Her husband is a petty officer stationed at Corry Station, but she drives past NAS Pensacola's main gate twice a day taking her daughter to Holy Spirit Catholic School on Navy Boulevard.

She had not heard about the plate readers until a reporter mentioned them.

"So they have my plate on file? For 90 days?" She paused. "I mean, I'm not doing anything wrong. But that's a lot of trust to ask from people who aren't even going on base."

The cameras at NAS Pensacola's three vehicle entry points capture plates from any vehicle that approaches within 50 feet of the checkpoint, according to a technical specifications document obtained through a Freedom of Information Act request. The system logs date, time, plate number and a photograph of the vehicle. It does not capture images of drivers or passengers.

Privacy advocates say the 90-day retention window is the central issue.

"The question isn't whether they're photographing plates at the gate—that's been happening for decades with human guards writing down plate numbers," said Marcus Webb, policy counsel at the Electronic Frontier Foundation, a digital rights organization. "The question is what happens to that data, who can access it, and how long it sits in a searchable database."

Webb said military installations have broad authority to conduct security screening. But he noted that data collected for one purpose often migrates to others.

"We've seen this pattern with fusion centers, with immigration enforcement, with civil asset forfeiture cases," he said. "Data collected at a military gate ends up in a police database, and suddenly it's being used to track someone's movements across town."

The efficiency tradeoff

Base officials emphasize that the primary purpose of the system is speed and security, not surveillance.

Before the upgrade, the main gate averaged wait times of 12 minutes during morning rush, with backups occasionally stretching to 25 minutes during shift changes or special events. Since January, average wait times have dropped to 3 minutes and 47 seconds, according to data provided by base security.

The system also allows security personnel to flag specific plates for enhanced screening without slowing the entire line. If a vehicle associated with a revoked access pass approaches, guards receive an alert before the driver reaches the checkpoint.

"This gives us options we didn't have before," said Master-at-Arms Chief Petty Officer David Liu, who oversees gate operations. "We can respond to a potential threat without turning every commuter into a suspect."

Liu said the overwhelming majority of plate data is never accessed by anyone. It sits in the database for 90 days, then automatically deletes.

"Unless there's an incident, unless there's a reason to pull it, nobody's looking at your plate," he said.

What the policy says

The Flightline requested a copy of the data retention and access policy governing the ALPR system. Base officials provided a two-page summary but declined to release the full policy document, citing operational security concerns.

The summary states that ALPR data may be accessed by "authorized security personnel for force protection purposes" and may be shared with "appropriate law enforcement agencies in accordance with applicable laws and regulations." It does not specify which agencies qualify or what approval process is required.

Escambia County Sheriff Chip Simmons said his office has not requested ALPR data from NAS Pensacola. He declined to say whether such requests might occur in the future.

"We have a good working relationship with base security," Simmons said. "If there's a situation where that data could help us solve a crime or protect public safety, we'd have that conversation."

Pensacola Police Department spokesperson Kayla Morrison said the department has "no formal data-sharing agreement" with NAS Pensacola regarding license plate readers.

A broader pattern

NAS Pensacola is one of 47 Navy installations scheduled to receive the Automated Installation Entry system by the end of 2027, according to Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command documents. The Army and Air Force are implementing similar systems at bases nationwide.

The expansion reflects a post-2019 security environment. In December of that year, a Saudi Arabian military trainee killed three sailors and injured eight others at NAS Pensacola before being shot by a deputy sheriff. The attack prompted a sweeping review of base security protocols and vetting procedures for foreign military students.

The AIE system was already in development before the shooting, but its rollout accelerated afterward. The technology is designed to detect anomalies—vehicles that appear at unusual times, plates that don't match registered information, patterns that suggest surveillance or pre-attack reconnaissance.

"Force protection is not static," Denton, the public affairs officer, wrote. "We have to adapt to evolving threats while maintaining an installation that functions as a workplace for thousands of people."

For workers and families who pass through those gates daily, the tradeoff between efficiency and privacy remains personal.

Santos, the contractor, said he appreciates the shorter lines. He also said he tries not to think too hard about the cameras.

"I figure they already know everything about me anyway," he said. "I've had a security clearance for 15 years. At this point, what's one more database?"

Kowalski is less sanguine. She said she plans to start taking a different route to her daughter's school.

"It's five extra minutes," she said. "But at least I know my car isn't ending up in some file somewhere."
